name: Amistad National Recreation Area
description: The 1969 Amistad Dam forms the massive Amistad Reservoir on the Rio Grande, creating a vast international lake that straddles the border between the United States and Mexico near Del Rio, Texas. This recreation area encompasses over 60,000 acres of water and surrounding desert landscape. Every fall, the area witnesses the spectacular migration of millions of monarch butterflies traveling south to Mexico, creating one of nature's most magnificent spectacles. The reservoir's shores support diverse wildlife including javelina (collared peccary), mouflon and aoudad sheep, blackbuck antelope, and numerous bird species. The exceptionally clear water of the reservoir provides excellent visibility for scuba diving, with beginner-friendly areas and complex underwater cave systems for advanced divers. Visitors can enjoy boating, fishing for bass and catfish, camping, hiking, and wildlife observation in a unique borderlands environment where two countries and two ecosystems meet.

name: Arapaho National Recreation Area
photo: Lake Granby, adjacent to the town of the same name near the southwestern entrance to Rocky Mountain National Park, Colorado LCCN2015633701.tif
description: This recreation area lies immediately adjacent to Rocky Mountain National Park, encompassing five interconnected reservoirs that are part of the massive Colorado-Big Thompson Project, a major water diversion system. Lake Granby, the largest of the five reservoirs and Colorado's third-largest body of water, dominates the landscape and offers exceptional boating, fishing, and water sports. The other four reservoirs—Shadow Mountain, Monarch, Willow Creek, and Meadow Creek—provide additional recreational opportunities. The area is extremely popular for boating, world-class trout fishing, camping, hiking through alpine meadows and forests, and horseback riding on extensive trail networks. The lakes and surrounding forests support abundant wildlife, with ospreys and bald eagles frequently seen fishing the waters, along with elk, moose, deer, and numerous other Rocky Mountain species. The area's high elevation provides cool summer temperatures and spectacular mountain scenery. Part of Arapaho-Roosevelt National Forest, this NRA combines excellent water recreation with mountain wilderness experiences in one of Colorado's most scenic regions.

name: Curecanti National Recreation Area
description: This recreation area lies around three stunning reservoirs on the Gunnison River upriver of Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park: Blue Mesa Reservoir, Morrow Point Reservoir, and Crystal Reservoir. Blue Mesa is Colorado's largest body of water, offering world-class trout fishing, boating, and water sports. Morrow Point and Crystal are nestled in dramatic canyon settings accessible by hiking trails. All three reservoirs provide excellent camping, hiking opportunities, and spectacular mountain scenery. The area is popular for its diverse recreational activities and breathtaking views of the surrounding peaks.

name: Flaming Gorge National Recreation Area
description: The Flaming Gorge Reservoir, formed by the Flaming Gorge Dam on the Green River, was named for the brilliant red sandstone cliffs that characterize this spectacular canyon. The reservoir spans 91 miles through Wyoming and Utah, offering exceptional fishing for rainbow trout, kokanee salmon, and smallmouth bass. Several full-service marinas provide boat rentals, fuel, and supplies, while numerous boat-in campsites offer secluded waterfront camping. The surrounding area features hiking trails, rock climbing opportunities, and wildlife viewing for bighorn sheep, elk, and mule deer. Part of Ashley National Forest, this area combines stunning desert canyon scenery with excellent water-based recreation.

name: Glen Canyon National Recreation Area
description: The vast area surrounding Lake Powell, formed by the Glen Canyon Dam on the Colorado River, encompasses over 1.2 million acres of spectacular sandstone country where ancient rivers carved sweeping canyons through millions of years of geological history, leaving behind scores of towering buttes, majestic natural arches, and dramatically striated cliffs painted in shades of red, orange, and pink. Lake Powell, one of America's largest reservoirs, stretches for 186 miles through these canyons, creating a water wonderland perfect for houseboating, fishing, waterskiing, and exploring remote side canyons accessible only by boat. Iconic scenic vistas include the world-famous Horseshoe Bend, where the Colorado River makes a spectacular 270-degree turn visible from a stunning overlook; Hole in the Rock, an historic passageway used by Mormon pioneers; and Rainbow Bridge National Monument, one of the world's largest natural bridges. The area offers exceptional opportunities for boating, fishing, camping, hiking, photography, and rock climbing. The combination of clear blue water against the red rock canyon walls creates some of the most photographed landscapes in the American Southwest.

name: Lake Chelan National Recreation Area
description: Part of the spectacular North Cascades National Park Complex along with Ross Lake NRA, this recreation area is nestled around the remote Stehekin Valley at the north end of Lake Chelan, one of the deepest lakes in the United States at 1,486 feet. The area is accessible only by ferry traveling 55 miles up the narrow, fjord-like lake to the tiny community of Stehekin, by floatplane, or by backpacking along the Pacific Crest Trail, making it one of the most isolated destinations in the lower 48 states. The Stehekin Valley offers campgrounds, rustic cabins, a lodge, and limited services in a truly remote wilderness setting. Visitors can explore hiking trails leading to waterfalls, alpine lakes, and high mountain passes, fish for trout in the lake and streams, or simply enjoy the unparalleled solitude and pristine wilderness. The area is famous for its spectacular mountain scenery, including views of some of the most glaciated peaks in the Cascade Range. This NRA provides a rare opportunity to experience true backcountry isolation while still having access to basic amenities.

name: Lake Mead National Recreation Area
description: This massive recreation area surrounds Lake Mead, formed by the iconic Hoover Dam, and Lake Mohave, created by Davis Dam, encompassing over 1.5 million acres of stunning desert landscapes. The area features three major desert ecosystems—the Mojave, Great Basin, and Sonoran Deserts—each with distinct plant and animal communities. The landscape includes dramatic slot canyons, rugged mountain summits, and colorful sandstone bluffs carved by wind and water over millions of years. The two lakes and the free-flowing Colorado River provide exceptional opportunities for canoeing, powerboating, sailing, fishing for striped bass and other species, and world-class scuba diving, with access through nine full-service marinas and harbors. Hikers can explore trails leading to abandoned gold mines, historic sites, and the ruins of the formerly submerged town of St. Thomas, which was exposed when lake levels dropped. Nine designated wilderness areas protect pristine desert habitats around the lakes. A portion of the recreation area overlaps with Grand Canyon–Parashant National Monument, protecting additional significant geological and cultural resources. The area receives over 7 million visitors annually, making it one of the most popular recreation destinations in the National Park System.

name: Lake Meredith National Recreation Area
description: Lake Meredith, located on the Canadian River in the Texas Panhandle, serves as a vital water source and recreational oasis in the arid High Plains region. The recreation area offers five well-maintained hiking trails that showcase the unique shortgrass prairie ecosystem, 5 miles of designated mountain biking trails, designated ATV areas for off-road enthusiasts, and extensive horseback riding opportunities. The shortgrass prairie ecosystem supports a remarkable diversity of wildlife adapted to this harsh environment, including the iconic Texas horned lizard (horny toad), colorful collared lizards, the distinctive greater roadrunner, and the brilliantly colored painted bunting. The area is also home to pronghorn antelope, coyotes, bobcats, and numerous bird species. The adjacent Alibates Flint Quarries National Monument preserves the area's unique geology of agatized dolomite, a colorful stone that was quarried by Native Americans for over 10,000 years to make tools and weapons. Visitors can enjoy fishing, boating, camping, hiking, and exploring both natural and cultural history in this unique High Plains setting.

name: Lake Roosevelt National Recreation Area
description: Franklin D. Roosevelt Lake, created by the monumental Grand Coulee Dam—the largest power plant in the United States—extends for 150 scenic miles up both the Columbia and Spokane Rivers, creating one of the longest reservoirs in North America. The recreation area encompasses over 100,000 acres of water and surrounding land, offering exceptional recreational opportunities. Several developed campgrounds dot the shores opposite the Colville and Spokane Indian Reservations, providing excellent access for boating, fishing for salmon, trout, bass, and walleye, and water sports. The area preserves significant cultural and historical sites, including the 1847 St. Paul's Mission near the former Kettle Falls, which was one of the most important Native American fishing sites on the Columbia River before it was submerged. Fort Spokane, established in 1882, provides interpretation of the complex history involving Native American tribes, the U.S. Army, and missionaries during the region's settlement era. Visitors can explore hiking trails, visit historic sites, enjoy water recreation, and learn about the area's rich cultural heritage. The reservoir serves as a major recreation destination for the Pacific Northwest while honoring the history of the people who lived here for thousands of years.

name: Land Between The Lakes National Recreation Area
description: This unique recreation area occupies a large inland peninsula situated between 35 miles of the parallel reservoirs Lake Barkley (formed by Barkley Dam) and Kentucky Lake (created by Kentucky Dam), creating the largest inland peninsula in the United States. The area encompasses more than 300 miles of scenic shoreline and an extensive network of over 500 miles of multi-use trails suitable for hiking, horseback riding, and mountain biking. This mostly flat, heavily forested area features diverse ecosystems including woodlands, wetlands, and open grasslands. A highlight is the 700-acre Elk & Bison Prairie, a restored native grassland where visitors can observe free-ranging elk and bison herds that once roamed this region. The area also includes a historic working farm that demonstrates 19th-century agricultural practices, a state-of-the-art planetarium offering astronomy programs, and an interactive nature center with educational exhibits about local wildlife and ecosystems. The recreation area provides camping, fishing, hunting, wildlife observation, environmental education, and numerous family-friendly activities, making it one of the most comprehensive recreation destinations in the Southeast.

name: Ross Lake National Recreation Area
description: Part of the magnificent North Cascades National Park Complex along with Lake Chelan NRA, this recreation area surrounds three stunning reservoirs that are part of Seattle City Light's Skagit River Hydroelectric Project: Gorge Lake, Diablo Lake (famous for its striking turquoise color), and Ross Lake, which extends 24 miles north into British Columbia, Canada. The area encompasses over 117,000 acres of pristine mountain wilderness. There are many boat-in backcountry campsites accessible only by watercraft, offering ultimate seclusion and wilderness camping experiences. The surrounding mountains of the North Cascades, often called the "American Alps" due to their rugged, glacier-carved peaks, provide exceptional opportunities for rugged hiking, mountaineering, rock climbing, and backpacking. The area features dense coniferous forests, alpine meadows, hundreds of glaciers, and countless waterfalls. Visitors can enjoy fishing, boating, hiking to remote alpine lakes, wildlife viewing (including mountain goats and black bears), and experiencing some of the most spectacular mountain scenery in the United States. The recreation area showcases the remarkable engineering achievement of the hydroelectric project while preserving exceptional wilderness values.

name: Bighorn Canyon National Recreation Area
description: The massive Yellowtail Dam, completed in 1967, created Bighorn Lake on the Bighorn River, forming a 71-mile-long reservoir that winds through one of America's deepest canyons, with walls rising over 2,500 feet above the water. This spectacular canyon and lake provide exceptional opportunities for wildlife viewing, including bighorn sheep, mountain goats, elk, deer, and numerous bird species; extensive hiking trails that lead to scenic overlooks and historic sites; world-class boating through the dramatic canyon; and excellent trout fishing for rainbow, brown, and cutthroat trout. Visitors can tour four meticulously preserved historic ranches that tell the story of early ranching in the West, including the Ewing-Snell Ranch and the Lockhart Ranch. Cyclists can ride along routes near the prehistoric Bad Pass Trail, used by Native Americans for thousands of years to travel between the plains and mountains. Perhaps most famously, wild mustangs roam freely in the overlapping Pryor Mountains Wild Horse Range, where visitors can observe these iconic symbols of the American West in their natural habitat. The combination of dramatic canyon scenery, rich history, and abundant wildlife makes this one of the most unique recreation areas in the National Park System.

name: Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area
photo: Palisades West Trail - Chattahoochee River November 2018 3.jpg
description: This recreation area consists of fifteen distinct park units that tightly follow the Chattahoochee River for 48 scenic miles below the Buford Dam, creating a ribbon of protected green space through the metropolitan Atlanta area. Each unit features well-maintained hiking trails that wind through diverse habitats including forests, wetlands, and river bluffs. The Chattahoochee River offers excellent opportunities for floating downstream in canoes, kayaks, or rafts, with varying difficulty levels suitable for all skill levels. The river's cool, clear water is released from Lake Lanier, providing a refreshing escape from the Georgia heat. Visitors can enjoy fishing for trout and bass, picnicking, birdwatching, and wildlife observation. The area supports abundant wildlife including river otters, beavers, deer, and numerous bird species. This NRA provides critical green space and recreational opportunities for millions of people in one of the fastest-growing metropolitan areas in the United States.

name: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area
description: Originally intended to surround a reservoir formed by the Tocks Island Dam, it became a larger natural area around 40 miles of the free-flowing Delaware River when the project was canceled. Historic hamlets that would have been inundated are open for tours. A biking and hiking trail runs the length of it parallel to the river, as does a segment of the Appalachian Trail. Rock climbers can scale cliffs of the Delaware Water Gap.

name: Gauley River National Recreation Area
description: The Gauley River downstream of the Summersville Dam offers 25 miles of world-renowned whitewater rafting featuring challenging Class V rapids, making it one of the premier whitewater destinations in the eastern United States. An additional 5 miles of the Meadow River also provide exceptional whitewater opportunities. The best rafting occurs during six weekends each fall when controlled water releases from the dam create optimal conditions for experienced paddlers. The Gauley season, known as "Gauley Season," draws thrill-seekers from around the world to tackle famous rapids with names like "Lost Paddle," "Iron Ring," and "Pure Screaming Hell." Beyond the intense whitewater, the area offers several river access points for fishing, hiking trails along the river corridor, and scenic overlooks. The rugged canyon setting provides stunning fall foliage viewing, while the surrounding forests support diverse wildlife. This NRA showcases one of the most challenging and rewarding whitewater experiences available in the eastern United States.

name: Hells Canyon National Recreation Area
description: Hells Canyon is the deepest river gorge in North America, with the east rim reaching up to 8,043 feet above the Snake River. Whitewater rafters paddle downstream of the Hells Canyon Dam, and there are scenic vistas on rim trails. Historic sites include Nez Perce petroglyphs on canyon walls, gold mining piles, and homestead ranches. Part of Wallowa–Whitman, Nez Perce, and Payette National Forests.

name: Smith River National Recreation Area
photo: View of stream along scenic Route 101 in Northern California LCCN2013632305.tif
description: The Smith River and its extensive tributaries, comprising one of the longest National Wild and Scenic River systems in the country, flow through this spectacular portion of California's Klamath Mountains, creating exceptional opportunities for fishing, rafting, kayaking, and swimming in crystal-clear waters. The river system is renowned for its pristine water quality and outstanding salmon and steelhead fishing. A network of trails leads visitors through diverse ecosystems to unique botanical areas including rare Darlingtonia pitcher plant groves found in specialized serpentine bogs, along with other endemic plant communities found nowhere else. The area's geology, featuring serpentine soils, creates unique growing conditions that support rare and endangered plant species. The recreation area encompasses rugged mountains, deep canyons, dense forests, and wildflower meadows. Visitors can enjoy camping, hiking, wildlife viewing, and exploring the area's unique natural heritage. Part of Six Rivers National Forest, this NRA protects one of America's most pristine river systems and showcases the remarkable biodiversity of the Klamath Mountains.

name: Ashley Karst National Recreation and Geologic Area
description: Congress established this unique area as both a national recreation and geologic area to conserve the significant karst watershed and exceptional geologic features on the south slope of the Uinta Mountains in Utah. Karst landscapes are characterized by sinkholes, caves, underground drainage systems, and distinctive limestone formations created by the dissolution of soluble rocks. The area protects rare and sensitive karst ecosystems that are vital for water quality and support unique cave-adapted species. Visitors can explore the fascinating geology while enjoying hiking, camping, fishing, and wildlife viewing. The Ashley Creek and other watersheds in this area feature spectacular gorges and canyons. Part of Ashley National Forest, this designation recognizes both the recreational value and the important scientific and conservation significance of these geologic formations.

name: Ed Jenkins National Recreation Area
photo: Springer Mountain and Black Mountain seen from East Ellijay.jpg
description: Springer Mountain, marking the southern terminus of the legendary 2,190-mile Appalachian Trail, serves as the centerpiece of this stunning portion of Georgia's Blue Ridge Mountains. Every year, thousands of thru-hikers begin their journey to Maine from this iconic peak. The area's extensive forests protect critical headwater streams of the Chattahoochee River watershed, which provides drinking water for millions of people downstream. The diverse hardwood forests support rich biodiversity and offer excellent hiking opportunities on numerous trails, including segments of the Appalachian Trail and other scenic routes. The area features rugged mountain terrain, cascading streams, and panoramic views. Part of Chattahoochee–Oconee National Forest, this recreation area honors Ed Jenkins, a former U.S. Representative who championed wilderness conservation.

name: Jemez National Recreation Area
description: This recreation area encompasses part of the traditional homeland of the Jemez Pueblo people, sitting among spectacular red rock canyons, dramatic cliffs, and elevated mesas in northern New Mexico's Jemez Mountains. The landscape features colorful cottonwood and aspen trees that create stunning seasonal displays, standing sentinel over prime river fishing sites and extensive hiking trail networks. Natural hot springs provide opportunities for relaxation, while numerous waterfalls cascade through the canyons. Most developed campsites and recreational facilities are concentrated along the Jemez River near the massive Valles Caldera, an ancient supervolcano that created one of the world's largest volcanic calderas. The area offers fishing, hiking, camping, wildlife viewing, and cultural heritage sites. Part of Santa Fe National Forest, this NRA combines natural beauty with rich Native American history.

name: Moosalamoo National Recreation Area
description: This scenic recreation area around Mount Moosalamoo in Vermont's Green Mountains features an extensive network of hiking trails through mixed hardwood and conifer forests. The area includes the popular Moosalamoo Campground located on a peaceful mountain lake.

name: Mount Baker National Recreation Area
description: Hikers traverse the spectacular south slope of Mount Baker, a glaciated 10,781-foot stratovolcano, reaching alpine campsites and the historic Park Butte Fire Lookout with commanding views of the Cascade Range. Part of Mount Baker–Snoqualmie National Forest.

name: Mount Hood National Recreation Area
description: This area encompasses three sections south and east of Oregon's Mount Hood (11,249 feet). The largest section is traversed by the Pacific Crest Trail and historic Barlow Road, an original Oregon Trail route. Part of Mount Hood National Forest.

name: Mount Rogers National Recreation Area
description: This area centers around Mount Rogers, Virginia's highest point at 5,729 feet, offering 500+ miles of trails including the Appalachian Trail. Famous for wild ponies roaming the highlands. Part of George Washington and Jefferson National Forests.

name: Pine Ridge National Recreation Area
description: This pristine backcountry area is intentionally kept roadless, offering true wilderness through rolling hills covered in ponderosa pine forest in the Nebraska Sandhills. Part of Nebraska National Forest.

name: Rattlesnake National Recreation Area
description: Trails traverse the Rattlesnake Mountains with forested ridges, alpine lakes, and open slopes. The pine-spruce-fir ecosystem is home to elk, white-tailed deer, mountain goat, and grizzly bear. Part of Lolo National Forest.

name: Robert T. Stafford White Rocks National Recreation Area
description: This roadless part of the Green Mountains has Big Branch and Peru Peak Wilderness areas. The white rocks are Cheshire quartzite exposed by glaciers. Features 30 miles of Appalachian Trail and Vermont's Long Trail. Part of Green Mountain National Forest.

name: Sawtooth National Recreation Area
description: The jagged Sawtooth Range and White Cloud, Boulder, and Smoky Mountains have hundreds of alpine lakes. More than 700 miles of trails connect hikers to grasslands and forests. Part of Sawtooth, Salmon–Challis, and Boise National Forests.

name: Spruce Knob–Seneca Rocks National Recreation Area
description: Features Spruce Knob (West Virginia's highest point at 4,863 feet) and iconic Seneca Rocks, a 900-foot quartzite cliff renowned for technical rock climbing in the eastern United States. Part of Monongahela National Forest.

name: Winding Stair Mountain National Recreation Area
description: The dramatic Winding Stair Mountains ridge runs the length of this area. Traversed by the Talimena Scenic Drive, a National Scenic Byway with breathtaking overlooks and fall foliage. Part of Ouachita National Forest.

name: Boston Harbor Islands National Recreation Area
description: This area consists of thirty-four islands scattered throughout Boston Harbor. Seasonal ferries from downtown Boston provide access to Boston Light (America's second-oldest lighthouse) and Civil War-era Fort Warren on Georges Island.

name: Gateway National Recreation Area
description: This massive urban area consists of three segments around the Hudson–Raritan Estuary. At Sandy Hook, visitors tour Fort Hancock and Sandy Hook Lighthouse (country's oldest operating lighthouse, 1764). Jamaica Bay provides critical habitat for hundreds of bird species.

name: Golden Gate National Recreation Area
description: One of the most visited units in the National Park System (15+ million visitors annually), includes Alcatraz Island, the Presidio, Sutro Baths ruins, Fort Point, and Muir Woods National Monument with its towering coast redwoods.

name: Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area
description: This collaboration spans the Santa Monica Mountains, one of the last wild areas in metropolitan Los Angeles. The 70-mile Backbone Trail traverses the entire mountain range, providing exceptional access from beaches to peaks.

name: Chickasaw National Recreation Area
description: Originally Platt National Park (1906), featuring mineral springs donated by the Chickasaw people. The springs form beautiful Travertine Creek with waterfalls. Lake of the Arbuckles offers excellent bass fishing and water recreation.

name: Oregon Dunes National Recreation Area
description: The largest coastal sand dunes in North America, covering 7,000 acres along a 40-mile stretch. These massive dunes (some over 500 feet high) offer off-road vehicle areas, hiking trails, and critical habitat for western snowy plover. Part of Siuslaw National Forest.

name: Spring Mountains National Recreation Area
description: This area encompasses Mount Charleston (Nevada's highest at 11,916 feet), creating a sky island just 35 miles from Las Vegas. Summer offers alpine trails and ancient Bristlecone pines. Winter brings skiing at Lee Canyon Resort. Part of Humboldt–Toiyabe National Forest.

name: White Mountains National Recreation Area
description: This vast, remote area north of Fairbanks offers isolated hiking and backcountry camping. Beaver Creek, a National Wild and Scenic River, provides exceptional fishing and floating. Extensive winter trails connect remote public-use cabins for dog sledding and cross-country skiing.

name: Allegheny National Recreation Area
description: This area conserves pristine forests on both sides of the Allegheny Reservoir (formed by Kinzua Dam). An extensive network of trails winds through hardwood forests, with campsites providing waterfront camping, fishing, and boating. Part of Allegheny National Forest.

name: Grand Island National Recreation Area
description: Grand Island in Lake Superior, accessible only by ferry near Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore. This remote island features stunning sandy beaches, coastal cliffs, dense forests, and the historic Grand Island East Channel Lighthouse. Part of Hiawatha National Forest.

name: Whiskeytown–Shasta–Trinity National Recreation Area
description: This unique area, managed jointly by NPS and USFS, encompasses three units totaling 244,000 acres. Whiskeytown features four major waterfalls and Gold Rush history. Shasta Lake and Trinity Lake offer world-class fishing, houseboating, and watersports. Part of Shasta–Trinity National Forest.
